title: New York State Court of Appeals

infobox.value: [Court of Appeals Hall](/wiki/Court_of_Appeals_Hall "Court of Appeals Hall") [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), United States
[Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") (questions of federal or constitutional law only)
[New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division")
| [New York State Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state) "Judiciary of New York (state)")
Born | Start | Term ends | Mandatory retirement[[a]](#cite_note-15) | Appointer | Law school

is the [highest court](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") in the [Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state) "Judiciary of New York (state)") of the [State of New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").[[1]](#cite_note-Siegel-1) It consists of seven judges: the [chief judge](/wiki/Chief_Judge_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "Chief Judge of the New York Court of Appeals") and six associate judges, who are appointed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") and confirmed by the [state senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate") to 14-year terms.[[1]](#cite_note-Siegel-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The chief judge of the Court of Appeals also heads administration of the state's court system, and thus is also known as the chief judge of the State of New York.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Court of Appeals was founded in 1847 and is located in the [New York Court of Appeals Building](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Appeals_Building "New York Court of Appeals Building") in [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York").

Chief Judge of New York. The Chief Judge supervises the seven-judge Court of Appeals and is chair of the [Administrative Board of the Courts](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state)#Administration "Judiciary of New York (state)").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8) In addition, the Chief Judge establishes standards and administrative policies after consultation with the Administrative Board and approval by the Court of Appeals.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8) The [Chief Administrator](/wiki/Chief_Administrator_of_the_Courts "Chief Administrator of the Courts") (or Chief Administrative Judge if a judge) is appointed by the Chief Judge with the advice and consent of the Administrative Board and oversees the administration and operation of the court system, assisted by the [Office of Court Administration](/wiki/New_York_State_Office_of_Court_Administration "New York State Office of Court Administration").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004131-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The eleven-member [New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct](/wiki/New_York_State_Commission_on_Judicial_Conduct "New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct") receives complaints, investigates, and makes initial determinations regarding judicial conduct and may recommend admonition, censure, or removal from office to the Chief Judge and Court of Appeals.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004132-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004133–134-12) The Court of Appeals promulgates rules for [admission](/wiki/Admission_to_the_bar_in_the_United_States "Admission to the bar in the United States") to [practice law](/wiki/Practice_of_law "Practice of law") in New York.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004135-13) (The [New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division") is responsible for actual admissions.[[14]](#cite_note-14)) The New York State Reporter is the official [reporter of decisions](/wiki/Reporter_of_decisions "Reporter of decisions") and is appointed by the Court of Appeals.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004153-7)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [New York State Court of Appeals](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Court_of_Appeals&redirect=no "New York State Court of Appeals"))

Highest court in the U.S. state of New York

| New York Court of Appeals | |
| --- | --- |
| Seal of the New York Court of Appeals | |
| Established | 1847 (1847) |
| Jurisdiction | [State of New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") |

The **New York Court of Appeals** is the [highest court](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") in the [Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state) "Judiciary of New York (state)") of the [State of New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)").[[1]](#cite_note-Siegel-1) It consists of seven judges: the [chief judge](/wiki/Chief_Judge_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "Chief Judge of the New York Court of Appeals") and six associate judges, who are appointed by the [governor](/wiki/Governor_of_New_York "Governor of New York") and confirmed by the [state senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate") to 14-year terms.[[1]](#cite_note-Siegel-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The chief judge of the Court of Appeals also heads administration of the state's court system, and thus is also known as the chief judge of the State of New York.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The Court of Appeals was founded in 1847 and is located in the [New York Court of Appeals Building](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Appeals_Building "New York Court of Appeals Building") in [Albany, New York](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York").

## Nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Nomenclature")]

New York uses an unusual nomenclature for its state courts. In the [federal court system](/wiki/Federal_judiciary_of_the_United_States "Federal judiciary of the United States") and in all other [U.S. states](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state"), the [court of last resort](/wiki/Court_of_last_resort "Court of last resort") is known as the "Supreme Court". New York, however, calls its lower courts the "[Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court "New York State Supreme Court")". In New York, the "Supreme Court" consists of the trial court and the intermediate appellate court, which is called the "Appellate Division" of the Supreme Court. The highest New York state court is called the Court of Appeals.[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESiegelConnors199817-4)

Further adding to the confusion is New York's terminology for jurists on its top two courts. Those who sit on its Supreme Court are referred to as "Justices" – the title reserved in most states and the Federal court system for members of the highest court – whereas the members of New York's highest court, the Court of Appeals, are simply called "Judges".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Jurisdiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Jurisdiction")]

[![A room with ornate brown wooden paneling and oil portraits on the walls. At the left seven people wearing black robes sit behind a similarly decorated wooden bench, elevated slightly from the red-carpeted floor. On the right are several people in suits sitting at chairs behind tables. In the rear is a large window with red drapes.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/New_York_Court_of_Appeals_hearing_oral_arguments.jpg/250px-New_York_Court_of_Appeals_hearing_oral_arguments.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_Court_of_Appeals_hearing_oral_arguments.jpg)

The Court sits in [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"). Here it hears oral arguments in a 2009 case over the [Atlantic Yards](/wiki/Atlantic_Yards "Atlantic Yards") development in Brooklyn.

Appeals are taken from the four departments of the [New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division") to the Court of Appeals. In some cases, an appeal lies of right, but in most cases, permission (or "leave") to appeal must be obtained, either from the Appellate Division itself or from the Court of Appeals. In civil cases, the Appellate Division panel or Court of Appeals votes on petitions for leave to appeal; in most criminal cases, however, the petition for leave to appeal is referred to a single Justice or Judge, whose decision whether to grant or deny leave is final. In some criminal cases, some appellate decisions by an Appellate Term or County Court are also appealable to the Court of Appeals, either of right or by permission.

In a few cases, an appeal can be taken from the court of first instance to the Court of Appeals, bypassing the Appellate Division. Direct appeals are authorized from final trial-court decisions in civil cases where the only issue is the constitutionality of a federal or state statute. In criminal cases, a direct appeal to the Court of Appeals is mandatory where a death sentence is imposed, but this provision has been irrelevant since New York's death-penalty law was declared unconstitutional. Decisions by the court can be appealed to the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") but only in cases containing a point of federal or constitutional law.

Decisions from the Court of Appeals are [binding authority](/wiki/Precedent "Precedent") on all lower courts, and persuasive authority for itself in later cases.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Every opinion, memorandum, and motion of the Court of Appeals sent to the New York State Reporter is required to be published in the *New York Reports*.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004149-6)[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004153-7)

## Administration of the courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Administration of the courts")]

|  |
| --- |
| Part of a series on |
| [New York State Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state) "Judiciary of New York (state)") |
|  |
| * Court of Appeals (highest) * [Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division") ([1st](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division,_First_Department "New York State Supreme Court, Appellate Division, First Department"), [2nd](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division,_Second_Department "New York State Supreme Court, Appellate Division, Second Department"), 3rd, [4th](/wiki/New_York_State_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division,_Fourth_Department "New York State Supreme Court, Appellate Division, Fourth Department")) * [Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") (1st level)  * [County Court](/wiki/New_York_county_courts "New York county courts") * [District Court](/wiki/New_York_district_courts "New York district courts") * New York City: [Civil](/wiki/New_York_City_Civil_Court "New York City Civil Court"), [Criminal](/wiki/New_York_City_Criminal_Court "New York City Criminal Court") * [Justice courts](/wiki/New_York_justice_courts "New York justice courts") |
| Specialized   * [Court of Claims](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Claims "New York Court of Claims") * [Surrogate's Court](/wiki/New_York_Surrogate%27s_Court "New York Surrogate's Court") * [Family Court](/wiki/New_York_Family_Court "New York Family Court") |
| * [v](/wiki/Template:New_York_State_Judiciary "Template:New York State Judiciary") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:New_York_State_Judiciary "Template talk:New York State Judiciary") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:New_York_State_Judiciary "Special:EditPage/Template:New York State Judiciary") |

The [New York State Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York "Judiciary of New York") is a unified state court system that functions under the [Chief Judge of the New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/Chief_Judge_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "Chief Judge of the New York Court of Appeals") who is the **[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio "Ex officio")** Chief Judge of New York. The Chief Judge supervises the seven-judge Court of Appeals and is chair of the [Administrative Board of the Courts](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York_(state)#Administration "Judiciary of New York (state)").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8) In addition, the Chief Judge establishes standards and administrative policies after consultation with the Administrative Board and approval by the Court of Appeals.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8) The [Chief Administrator](/wiki/Chief_Administrator_of_the_Courts "Chief Administrator of the Courts") (or Chief Administrative Judge if a judge) is appointed by the Chief Judge with the advice and consent of the Administrative Board and oversees the administration and operation of the court system, assisted by the [Office of Court Administration](/wiki/New_York_State_Office_of_Court_Administration "New York State Office of Court Administration").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004130-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004131-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The eleven-member [New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct](/wiki/New_York_State_Commission_on_Judicial_Conduct "New York State Commission on Judicial Conduct") receives complaints, investigates, and makes initial determinations regarding judicial conduct and may recommend admonition, censure, or removal from office to the Chief Judge and Court of Appeals.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004132-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004133–134-12) The Court of Appeals promulgates rules for [admission](/wiki/Admission_to_the_bar_in_the_United_States "Admission to the bar in the United States") to [practice law](/wiki/Practice_of_law "Practice of law") in New York.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004135-13) (The [New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division") is responsible for actual admissions.[[14]](#cite_note-14)) The New York State Reporter is the official [reporter of decisions](/wiki/Reporter_of_decisions "Reporter of decisions") and is appointed by the Court of Appeals.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGibsonManz2004153-7)

## Judges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Judges")]

For lists including past judges, see [List of chief judges of the New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/List_of_chief_judges_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "List of chief judges of the New York Court of Appeals") and [List of associate judges of the New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/List_of_associate_judges_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "List of associate judges of the New York Court of Appeals").

| Name | Born | Start | Term ends | Mandatory retirement[[a]](#cite_note-15) | Appointer | Law school |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Rowan D. Wilson](/wiki/Rowan_D._Wilson "Rowan D. Wilson"), *Chief Judge* | (1960-09-03) September 3, 1960 (age 65) | February 6, 2017[[b]](#cite_note-16) | December 31, 2030[[c]](#cite_note-Ret-17) | | [Kathy Hochul](/wiki/Kathy_Hochul "Kathy Hochul") (D)[[d]](#cite_note-18) | [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_Law_School "Harvard Law School") |
| [Jenny Rivera](/wiki/Jenny_Rivera_(judge) "Jenny Rivera (judge)") | (1960-12-08) December 8, 1960 (age 65) | February 11, 2013 | February 10, 2027 | December 31, 2030 | [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") (D) | [NYU](/wiki/New_York_University_School_of_Law "New York University School of Law") |
| [Michael J. Garcia](/wiki/Michael_J._Garcia "Michael J. Garcia") | (1961-10-08) October 8, 1961 (age 64) | February 8, 2016 | February 7, 2030 | December 31, 2031 | [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") (D) | [Albany](/wiki/Albany_Law_School "Albany Law School") |

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

[![A three-story light-colored stone building. In the front a pedimented central pavilion with six Ionic columns projects. Between the second and third stories of the main facade there is a large molded cornice.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/CourtofAppealsPano.jpg/250px-CourtofAppealsPano.jpg)](/wiki/File:CourtofAppealsPano.jpg)

The 1842 courthouse of the New York Court of Appeals in [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), Henry Rector, architect

The Court of Appeals was created by the [New York State Constitution](/wiki/New_York_State_Constitution "New York State Constitution") of 1846 to replace both the [Court for the Correction of Errors](/wiki/New_York_Court_for_the_Trial_of_Impeachments "New York Court for the Trial of Impeachments") and the [Court of Chancery](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Chancery "New York Court of Chancery"), and had eight members. Four judges were elected by general ballot at the State elections, the other four were chosen annually from among the [Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") justices. The first four judges elected at the [special judicial state election](/wiki/New_York_special_judicial_election,_1847 "New York special judicial election, 1847") in June 1847 were [Freeborn G. Jewett](/wiki/Freeborn_G._Jewett "Freeborn G. Jewett") (to a term of two and a half years), [Greene C. Bronson](/wiki/Greene_C._Bronson "Greene C. Bronson") (to a term of four and a half years), [Charles H. Ruggles](/wiki/Charles_H._Ruggles "Charles H. Ruggles") (to a term of six and a half years), and [Addison Gardiner](/wiki/Addison_Gardiner "Addison Gardiner") (to a term of eight and a half years). They took office on July 5, 1847. Afterwards, every two years, one judge was elected in odd-numbered years to an eight-year term.[[15]](#cite_note-19) In case of a vacancy, a judge was temporarily appointed by the Governor,[[16]](#cite_note-20) and at the next odd-year state election a judge was elected for the remainder of the term.[[17]](#cite_note-21) The Chief Judge was always that one of the elected judges who had the shortest remaining term. Besides, the Court had a [Clerk](/wiki/Clerk_of_the_New_York_Court_of_Appeals "Clerk of the New York Court of Appeals") who was elected to a three-year term.

In [1869](/wiki/New_York_state_election,_1869 "New York state election, 1869"), the proposed new State Constitution was rejected by the voters. Only the "Judicial Article", which re-organized the New York Court of Appeals, was adopted by a small majority, with 247,240 for and 240,442 against it. The Court of Appeals was wholly re-organised, taking effect on July 4, 1870. All sitting judges were legislated out of office, and seven new judges were elected by general ballot at a [special election on May 17, 1870](/wiki/New_York_special_judicial_election,_1870 "New York special judicial election, 1870").[[18]](#cite_note-22) Democrat [Sanford E. Church](/wiki/Sanford_E._Church "Sanford E. Church") defeated Republican [Henry R. Selden](/wiki/Henry_R._Selden "Henry R. Selden") for Chief Judge. The tickets for associate judges had only four names each and the voters could cast only four ballots, so that four judges were chosen by the majority[[19]](#cite_note-23) and two by the minority.[[20]](#cite_note-24) [Martin Grover](/wiki/Martin_Grover "Martin Grover") was the only sitting judge who was re-elected. The judges were elected to a 14-year term, which most judges did not complete, since the Constitution mandated the retirement of the judges at the end of the calendar year in which they reached the age of 70. In case of a vacancy due to death or resignation, a judge was appointed by the Governor until a successor was chosen at the next State election. To replace retiring or appointed judges, all substitutes were elected to full 14-year terms.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG/250px-NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG)](/wiki/File:NYSCourtofAppeals1.JPG)

A view of the courthouse's neoclassical [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico")

In 1889, a "Second Division" of the Court of Appeals was established temporarily to help to decide the large number of cases. Its seven members were designated[[21]](#cite_note-25) by Governor [David B. Hill](/wiki/David_B._Hill "David B. Hill"), chosen from the [New York Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court")'s General Term benches. Chief Judge was [Daniel L. Follett](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_L._Follett&action=edit&redlink=1 "Daniel L. Follett (page does not exist)"). Among its members were [Alton B. Parker](/wiki/Alton_B._Parker "Alton B. Parker") and Joseph Potter.[[22]](#cite_note-26) The Second Division was continued through 1890.[[23]](#cite_note-27) In 1891, the State Constitutional Commission, headed by [William B. Hornblower](/wiki/William_B._Hornblower "William B. Hornblower") drafted an amendment to abolish the Second Division.[[24]](#cite_note-28)

A constitutional amendment adopted in November 1899 permitted the Governor, at the request of a majority of the judges of the Court of Appeals, to designate up to four justices of the Supreme Court to serve as associate judges of the Court of Appeals until the Court's calendar was reduced below two hundred cases. This goal was reached only in 1921, and henceforth no more Supreme Court justices were designated under the amendment of 1899 to serve on the Court of Appeals.

[Jacob D. Fuchsberg](/wiki/Jacob_D._Fuchsberg "Jacob D. Fuchsberg") and [Lawrence H. Cooke](/wiki/Lawrence_H._Cooke "Lawrence H. Cooke") were the last judges elected by general ballot at the State election in November 1974. Afterwards the judges have been nominated by the Governor and confirmed by the [New York State Senate](/wiki/New_York_State_Senate "New York State Senate").

## Notable cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Notable cases")]

The Court of Appeals has decided some of the most important cases in American jurisprudence.[[25]](#cite_note-29)

### Conflict of laws

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Conflict of laws")]

* *[Babcock v. Jackson](/wiki/Babcock_v._Jackson "Babcock v. Jackson")* ([Fuld](/wiki/Stanley_H._Fuld "Stanley H. Fuld"), J.): holding that the law of the jurisdiction governs that has the strongest interest in the resolution of the particular issue presented.

### Statutory interpretation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Statutory interpretation")]

* *[Riggs v. Palmer](/wiki/Riggs_v._Palmer "Riggs v. Palmer")* ([Earl](/wiki/Robert_Earl_(judge) "Robert Earl (judge)"), J): used the "[social purpose](/wiki/Social_purpose "Social purpose")" rule of [statutory construction](/wiki/Statutory_construction "Statutory construction"), the process of interpreting a will.

### Contracts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Contracts")]

* *[Wood v. Lucy, Lady Duff-Gordon](/wiki/Wood_v._Lucy,_Lady_Duff-Gordon "Wood v. Lucy, Lady Duff-Gordon")* ([Cardozo](/wiki/Benjamin_N._Cardozo "Benjamin N. Cardozo"), J.): was both a minor *[cause célèbre](/wiki/Cause_c%C3%A9l%C3%A8bre "Cause célèbre")* at the time and an influential development in the law of contract [consideration](/wiki/Consideration "Consideration").
* *[Jacob & Youngs v. Kent](/wiki/Jacob_%26_Youngs_v._Kent "Jacob & Youngs v. Kent")* (Cardozo, J.): held that expectation damages arising from a breach of contract are limited to the diminution of the property's value if the undoing of the breach was an economic waste.
* *[Boomer v. Atlantic Cement Co.](/wiki/Boomer_v._Atlantic_Cement_Co. "Boomer v. Atlantic Cement Co.")* ([Bergan](/wiki/Francis_Bergan "Francis Bergan"), J.): the court granted an injunction against the cement plant for nuisance, but permitted the plant to pay permanent damages after which the court would vacate the injunction. In essence, the court permitted the plant to pay the net present value of its effects and to continue polluting.

### Corporations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Corporations")]

* *[Berkey v. Third Avenue Railway Co](/wiki/Berkey_v._Third_Avenue_Railway_Co "Berkey v. Third Avenue Railway Co")* (Cardozo, J.): held that the Third Avenue Railway Co was not liable for the debts of the subsidiary. It was necessary that the domination of the parent company over the subsidiary was required to be complete, in order for the parent company to be treated as liable for the debts of the subsidiary. It was needed that the subsidiary be merely the alter ego of the parent, or that the subsidiary be thinly capitalized, so as to perpetrate a fraud on the creditors.
* *[Meinhard v. Salmon](/wiki/Meinhard_v._Salmon "Meinhard v. Salmon")* (Cardozo, J.): held that managing partner in a joint venture had a fiduciary duty to inform the investing partner of an opportunity that would arise after the scheduled termination of the partnership.
* *[Walkovszky v. Carlton](/wiki/Walkovszky_v._Carlton "Walkovszky v. Carlton")* ([Fuld](/wiki/Stanley_Fuld "Stanley Fuld"), J.): refused to pierce the veil on account of undercapitalization alone.

### Criminal law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Criminal law")]

* *[People v. Molineux](/wiki/People_v._Molineux "People v. Molineux")* ([Werner](/wiki/William_E._Werner "William E. Werner"), J.): held that using 'evidence' of an unproven previous act of murder against the defendant in a subsequent unrelated trial violated the basic tenet of presumption of innocence, and, therefore, such evidence was inadmissible
* *[People v. Onofre](/wiki/New_York_v._Onofre "New York v. Onofre")* ([Jones](/wiki/Hugh_R._Jones "Hugh R. Jones")): held that it is not the function of the penal law to provide for the enforcement of moral or theological values.
* *[People v. Antommarchi](/wiki/Antommarchi_Rights "Antommarchi Rights")* ([Simons](/wiki/Richard_D._Simons "Richard D. Simons"), J.): affirming the statutory rights of a defendant to be present during any sidebar questioning of a prospective juror concerning his or her impartiality.
* *[People v. Goetz](/wiki/People_v._Goetz "People v. Goetz")* ([Wachtler](/wiki/Sol_Wachtler "Sol Wachtler"), CJ): held that 1) The defense of justification, which permits the use of deadly physical force, is not a purely subjective standard; the actor must not only have the subjective belief that deadly physical force is necessary, but those beliefs must also be objectively reasonable. 2) The mere appearance of perjured testimony given before the Grand Jury is not sufficient to sustain a dismissal of an indictment.
* *[People v. Scott](/wiki/Open-fields_doctrine#People_v._Scott "Open-fields doctrine")* (Hancock, J.), held that the protections against unlawful search and seizure in the state constitution are broad enough that, contrary to the [open-fields doctrine](/wiki/Open-fields_doctrine "Open-fields doctrine") affirmed by the U.S. Supreme Court in *[Oliver v. United States](/wiki/Oliver_v._United_States "Oliver v. United States")*, a landowner can assert a [reasonable expectation of privacy](/wiki/Reasonable_expectation_of_privacy "Reasonable expectation of privacy") against a [warrantless search](/wiki/Warrantless_search "Warrantless search") of *all* property, not just that within the [curtilage](/wiki/Curtilage "Curtilage") of the house, as long as they have made some effort to exclude the public such as posting or fencing the property or gating roads. New York is one of five states where courts have declined to adopt the doctrine.
* *[People v. LaValle](/wiki/People_v._LaValle "People v. LaValle")* ([G.B Smith](/wiki/George_Bundy_Smith "George Bundy Smith"), J.): The current statute of capital punishment in the state of New York was unconstitutional as it violated article one, section six of the state constitution.

### Torts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Torts")]

* *[Devlin v. Smith](/wiki/Devlin_v._Smith "Devlin v. Smith")*: The Court held that a duty to third parties "exists when a defect is such as to render the article in itself imminently dangerous, and serious injury to any person using it is a natural and probable consequence of its use." The Court further held that scaffolding to be used in the painting of a courthouse was an inherently dangerous article.
* *[Schloendorff v. Society of New York Hospital](/wiki/Schloendorff_v._Society_of_New_York_Hospital "Schloendorff v. Society of New York Hospital")* (Cardozo, J.): established principles of informed consent and respondeat superior in United States law
* *[MacPherson v. Buick Motor Co.](/wiki/MacPherson_v._Buick_Motor_Co. "MacPherson v. Buick Motor Co.")* (Cardozo, J.): helped signal the end of the law's attachment with [privity](/wiki/Privity "Privity") as a source of duty in [products liability](/wiki/Products_liability "Products liability"). This is the foundational doctrine underlying nearly all modern [product liability](/wiki/Product_liability "Product liability") lawsuits.
* *[Palsgraf v. Long Island Railroad Co.](/wiki/Palsgraf_v._Long_Island_Railroad_Co. "Palsgraf v. Long Island Railroad Co.")* (Cardozo, J.): was important in the development of the concept of the [proximate cause](/wiki/Proximate_cause "Proximate cause") in tort law.
* *[Martin v. Herzog](/wiki/Martin_v._Herzog "Martin v. Herzog")* (Cardozo, J.): holding that the unexcused violation of a statutory duty is [negligence per se](/wiki/Negligence_per_se "Negligence per se") and a jury does not have the power to relax the duty that one traveler on the highway owes under a statute to another on the same highway.
* *[Chysky v. Drake Bros. Co.](/wiki/Chysky_v._Drake_Bros._Co. "Chysky v. Drake Bros. Co.")* ([McLaughlin](/wiki/Chester_B._McLaughlin "Chester B. McLaughlin"), J.): The Court held that a plaintiff cannot recover from a defendant based on implied warranty when she does not have contractual privity with him; thus, a plaintiff cannot recover from a defendant who sold her employer food unfit for consumption, because the defendant's implied warranty extended only to the employer.
* *[Tedla v. Ellman](/wiki/Tedla_v._Ellman "Tedla v. Ellman")* ([Lehman](/wiki/Irving_Lehman "Irving Lehman"), J.): the court held that because the violation occurred in a situation not anticipated by the drafters of the statute and was in keeping with the spirit of the statute, it did not constitute negligence.
* *[Akins v. Glens Falls City School District](/wiki/Baseball_Rule#Akins_v._Glens_Falls_City_School_District "Baseball Rule")* ([Jasen](/wiki/Matthew_J._Jasen "Matthew J. Jasen"), J.): The Court held that the [Baseball Rule](/wiki/Baseball_Rule "Baseball Rule"), an exception to tort law under which spectators at sporting events cannot hold teams, players or venues liable if they are injured by a ball leaving the field as long as some protected seating was available, is still valid under [comparative negligence](/wiki/Comparative_negligence "Comparative negligence"), the first time it was challenged under that doctrine.
* *Trimarco v. Klein* ([Fuchsberg](/wiki/Jacob_D._Fuchsberg "Jacob D. Fuchsberg"), J.): held that custom and usage is highly relevant evidence related to the reasonable person standard but it does not per se define the scope of negligence.

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_Court_of_Appeals&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Further reading")]
